personality_voice.book_baseline: Dennis is defined by fearless, almost absurd enthusiasm. He falls in the lake during his arrival and treats it as a highlight rather than a fright. He stands on chairs to see the Beauxbatons carriage, guesses 'it's a flying house' with cheerful confidence, and shrieks with delight rather than fear. His voice is shrill, excitable, and physically expressive. He is the younger, even more unguarded version of Colin's hero-worship energy — but where Colin fixates on Harry, Dennis fixates on everything with equal wonder.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film corpus is entirely empty. Dennis Creevey does not appear in any film scene provided.
personality_voice.difference: Every personality trait — the fearless excitement, the shrill voice, the physical comedy of being too small for Hagrid's coat, the double thumbs-up, the delight at near-drowning — is entirely absent from the films.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: A score of 0–5 applies when the character is unrecognizable. Dennis is not merely simplified; he is absent. The score reflects near-total absence rather than misrepresentation, but the effect on faithfulness is the same: nothing of his book personality is conveyed.
personality_voice.evidence_status: Directly evidenced (book); Not enough evidence (film — no film corpus provided)

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Dennis functions as a minor but recurring presence: a living emblem of wide-eyed Hogwarts wonder, a foil to Colin, and a small source of comic texture. He joins the DA in Order of the Phoenix, placing him among those who actively resist Umbridge. His agency is modest but real: he chooses to attend, reacts independently, and contributes to crowd scenes and atmosphere.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: No film appearances are present in the corpus.
narrative_role_agency.difference: His entire narrative function — comic texture, DA membership, sibling dynamic with Colin — is absent.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Score of 0–5 applies when role and agency are eliminated or made functionally irrelevant. Dennis has no presence in the films from which to evaluate agency.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Dennis's motivation is simple but consistent: pure, unself-conscious delight in the magical world. He is not conflicted — he is a character defined by the absence of fear or hesitation. His joining the DA suggests a developing loyalty to Harry and resistance to authority, but this is not developed in the corpus provided.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: No motivations are conveyed in the films because the character does not appear.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Score of 0–4 applies when motivations are absent. With no film presence, nothing of his motivational texture is communicated.

character_arc.book_baseline: Dennis has a flat arc in the books — he arrives as an enthusiastic first year and remains essentially that throughout. His most significant development is joining the DA, which implies growing courage and loyalty, but the corpus does not show a transformation arc. The arc is intentionally minor.
character_arc.difference: Even a flat arc requires the character to exist on screen. He does not.
character_arc.penalty_logic: A flat arc faithfully preserved scores 14–15. A flat arc that is entirely absent scores near 0. The character cannot be said to have any arc in the films.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Dennis's primary relationship is with Colin — they share a sibling bond built on mutual excitement, with Colin as the slightly more knowing older brother and Dennis as the even more unfiltered version. The double thumbs-up and 'I fell in!' exchange show warmth, shared enthusiasm, and a dynamic where Colin contextualizes the magical world for Dennis ('It was probably the giant squid'). A secondary relationship exists with Harry, mediated through Colin's hero-worship.
key_relationships.difference: The Colin-Dennis sibling dynamic, which is one of the warmer minor-character relationships in the books, is entirely absent.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: Score of 0–2 applies when key relationships are absent. The sibling dynamic is the core of Dennis's relational identity and it does not exist in the films.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Dennis's complexity is modest but specific: he is not merely 'excited kid' — he is a child who treats genuine danger (falling into a dark lake, a giant squid) as a gift, which gives him a distinct comic and tonal texture. The image of him drowning in Hagrid's coat, giving a double thumbs-up, mouthing 'I fell in the lake!' is a small but precise characterization. His DA membership adds a layer of quiet courage beneath the silliness.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: All of this texture — the physical comedy, the fearlessness-as-character-trait, the sibling warmth, the DA courage — is lost entirely.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: Score of 0–4 applies when complexity is almost entirely removed. There is nothing in the films to preserve or lose; the character simply does not exist there.

lost_or_transferred_material: Dennis falling in the lake and treating it with delight — his defining introduction scene
The double thumbs-up and 'I fell in!' exchange with Colin at the Sorting
Dennis drowning in Hagrid's moleskin coat (physical comedy and visual characterization)
Dennis standing on his chair to see the Beauxbatons carriage
Dennis guessing 'it's a flying house' — showing fearless, imaginative engagement
The Colin-Dennis sibling dynamic as a recurring warm presence
Dennis's membership in Dumbledore's Army (Order of the Phoenix)
Dennis as a recurring minor presence providing comic texture and wonder across multiple books
score_caps_applied: Cap 4 applies: the films preserve only the broadest identity (a Creevey brother exists) but remove essentially all of what defines Dennis's book characterization. Score cannot exceed 70. In practice the score is far below this threshold due to complete absence.
comparative.key_observations: Dennis Creevey does not appear in any film scene in the provided corpus, and based on general knowledge of the Harry Potter films, he is absent from the film adaptations entirely (unlike Colin, who does appear). This is a case of complete omission rather than misrepresentation. Every defining trait — his fearless enthusiasm, his physical comedy, his sibling dynamic with Colin, his DA membership — is lost not through compression but through non-existence. The score of 14 reflects near-total absence. Confidence is Medium rather than Low because the book corpus is reasonably rich for a minor character, giving a clear baseline; the uncertainty is not about what the books establish but about whether any film material exists that was not captured in the corpus. If Dennis truly has zero film presence, the score would be at the floor for most dimensions.
